train_pairs.target: Is it the thief who sings?
The dog is afraid of us.

train_pairs.source: na ǁu ǂʔii ŋ ɡǀa xaŋki
train_pairs.target: I do not think about your_{sg} mother.

train_pairs.source: ɡǀi aŋki si ǀqhõʕo ŋ ɡǃari
train_pairs.target: Will our father dance in Upington?
Is it me whom you_{sg} call?
It is you_{pl} whom the woman thinks about.

train_pairs.source: a xaŋki ke kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀa ts’aaʔa ŋ
train_pairs.target: Your_{sg} mother asks if you_{sg} like me.

train_pairs.source: kama ɟa xae ŋ si ŋǀii u ŋǁa
ŋ ǃuu ke kacuuʔi ŋ ŋǀŋ si ǂqheeke ŋ ǃhaeke
train_pairs.target: My grandfather asks if I will sing tomorrow.

test_pairs.source: na kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀu xae ŋ ǃuu ǃʔai
test_pairs.target: I ask if it is you_{pl} whom my grandfather calls.

test_pairs.source: na ǁu si sĩisen ŋ ɡǃari
test_pairs.target: I will not work in Upington.

test_pairs.source: ǀeeki ke ǃaukea ŋ ŋǀŋ ǂhun
test_pairs.target: The woman is afraid of my dog.
It is I who will call you_{sg}.

verification.sentence_tested: na kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀu xae ŋ ǃuu ǃʔai
verification.reference: I ask if it is you_{pl} whom my grandfather calls.
verification.hypothesis:
I'll attempt to translate the sentence. However, please note that the language appears to be !Xóõ, a language isolate spoken in Botswana. The translation may not be entirely accurate due to the complexity of the language.

The sentence "na kacuuʔi ŋ ɡǀu xae ŋ ǃuu ǃʔai" can be broken down into its components:

- "na" is a conjunction meaning "and" or "with"
- "kacuuʔi" is a noun, possibly referring to a person or a group
- "ŋ" is a prefix indicating a possessive relationship
- "ɡǀu" is a noun, possibly referring to a place or a location
- "xae" is a verb, possibly meaning "to go" or "to move"
- "ŋ" is a prefix indicating a possessive relationship
- "ǃuu" is a noun, possibly referring to a person or a group
- "ǃʔai" is a verb, possibly meaning "to see" or "to look at"

With this breakdown, a possible translation of the sentence could be:

"And the person from the place is going to see the other person."

However, this translation is highly speculative and may not accurately convey the intended meaning of the original sentence.

In the example ǂoo ŋǀii ŋ, I identify the morphemes as ǂoo (subject pronoun), ŋǀii (transitive verb), and ŋ (object marker). This example suggests that the object marker ŋ is used to indicate the object of the verb.
In the example ɡǀa ǃaukea ŋ ŋǀŋ, I identify the morphemes as ɡǀa (subject pronoun), ǃaukea (intransitive verb), ŋ (object marker), and ŋǀŋ (possessive marker). This example suggests that the possessive marker ŋǀŋ is used to indicate possession of the object.
In the example cuu xae a ǂʔii ŋǁa, I identify the morphemes as cuu (subject pronoun), xae (possessive marker), a (object pronoun or marker), ǂʔii (intransitive verb), and ŋǁa (suffix indicating a specific grammatical function). This example suggests that the suffix ŋǁa is used to indicate a question.
In the example u ǁãu ke ǁu ǀqhõʕo, I identify the morphemes as u (subject pronoun), ǁãu (possessive marker), ke (marker or pronoun), ǁu (object pronoun or marker), and ǀqhõʕo (transitive verb). This example suggests that the possessive marker ǁãu is used to indicate possession of the subject.
In the example ɟanasa xae ǂqheeke, I identify the morphemes as ɟanasa (noun or subject pronoun) and ǂqheeke (verb or phrase). This example suggests that the noun ɟanasa can function as a subject pronoun.
In the example ŋǀŋ si sĩisen ŋ ǃhaeke, I identify the morphemes as ŋǀŋ (possessive marker), si (marker or pronoun), sĩisen (intransitive verb), ŋ (object marker), and ǃhaeke (transitive verb). This example suggests that the possessive marker ŋǀŋ is used to indicate possession of the subject.
In the example ǂhun ke ǃaukea ŋ ɡǀi, I identify the morphemes as ǂhun (subject pronoun), ke (marker or pronoun), ǃaukea (intransitive verb), ŋ (object marker), and ɡǀi (object pronoun or marker). This example suggests that the object pronoun or marker ɡǀi is used to indicate the object of the verb.
In the example na ǁu ǂʔii ŋ ɡǀa xaŋki, I identify the morphemes as na (subject pronoun or marker), ǁu (object pronoun or marker), ǂʔii (intransitive verb), ŋ (object marker), ɡǀa (subject pronoun), and xaŋki (object pronoun or marker). This example suggests that the object pronoun or marker ǁu is used to indicate the object of the verb.
In the example ɡǀi aŋki si ǀqhõʕo ŋ ɡǃari, I identify the morphemes as ɡǀi (object pronoun or marker), aŋki (marker or pronoun), si (marker or pronoun), ǀqhõʕo (transitive verb), ŋ (object marker), and ɡǃari (object pronoun or marker). This example suggests that the object pronoun or marker ɡǀi is used to indicate the object of the verb.
In the example kiɟa xae ǂoo sĩisen ŋǁa, I identify the morphemes as kiɟa (marker or pronoun), xae (possessive marker), ǂoo (subject pronoun), sĩisen (intransitive verb), ŋ (object marker), and ŋǁa (suffix indicating a specific grammatical function). This example suggests that the suffix ŋǁa is used to indicate a question.
In the example ɡǀa ŋǀii a ŋǁŋ, I identify the morphemes as ɡǀa (subject pronoun), ŋǀii (transitive verb), a (object pronoun or marker), and ŋǁŋ (possessive marker). This example suggests that the possessive marker ŋǁŋ is used to indicate possession of the object.
In the example ŋǀŋ xae a ǃʔai, I identify the morphemes as ŋǀŋ (possessive marker), xae (possessive marker), a (object pronoun or marker), and ǃʔai (intransitive verb). This example suggests that the possessive marker xae is used to indicate possession of the object.
In the example u kee ǀeeki ǂʔii ŋǁa, I identify the morphemes as u (subject pronoun), kee (marker or pronoun), ǀeeki (object pronoun or marker), ǂʔii (intransitive verb), and ŋǁa (suffix indicating a specific grammatical function). steps.accumulated_dict:
- ǂʔii: about, concerning (preposition)
- ǀqhõʕo: not (negation marker)
- ke: does not, negation marker (alternative to ǀqhõʕo)
- ŋǁa: do, perform (verb)
- ŋǀŋ: of, from, preposition (indicates possession or origin)
- ǃhaeke: will, future tense (verb)
- si: time period, tomorrow (noun)
- u: you (plural), pronoun (alternative to ɡǀa)
- kacuuʔi: if, question marker
- ts’aaʔa: like, enjoy (verb)
- ɟa: when, time (adverb)
- ŋ: my, possessive pronoun (alternative to ɡǀa)
- ǃuu: grandfather (noun)
- ǂqheeke: sings (verb)
- ǃhaeke: will, future tense (verb)
- kama: when, time (adverb)
- xae: you (singular), pronoun (alternative to ɡǀa)
- ŋǀii: see, perceive (verb)
- ǃuu: grandfather (noun)
- ǂoo sĩisen: man works (compound noun, alternative to ǂoo)

- The word si may be a noun indicating 'tomorrow' or a time period.
